Highlights
----------

*   •[Biofertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biofertilizer "Learn more about Biofertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology is a viable alternative to toxic chemical fertilizers. 
*   •Erratic supplies and frivolous quality control limit this technology's adoption. 
*   •Use of nanotechnology for the development of most effective biofertilizers. 
*   •Green synthesis or nanocoating/encapsulation of useful microbes by [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 
*   •Bio-nanofertilizers increase crops [nutrient use efficiency](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nutrient-use-efficiency "Learn more about nutrient use efficiency from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). 

Abstract
--------

Sustainability and innovation are fundamental to any production system. Providing food to the increasing population is of paramount importance and is crtical to the global stability. However, the issue of food security is facing stiff challenge from continuously declining soil fertility and scarcity of fertile land. Our reliance on excessive use of toxic [chemical fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chemical-fertilizer "Learn more about chemical fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for increased yield is major concerns for environment and [agriculture's](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture's from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") future. [Biofertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/biofertilizer "Learn more about Biofertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are gaining traction as a viable alternative to toxic [chemical fertilizers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/chemical-fertilizer "Learn more about chemical fertilizers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), with a market potential of 3.8 billion dollars by 2025. Use of biofertilizer is natural mean to use live microorganisms to improve soil nutritional status by root [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") expansion, enhanced nutrient availability and acquisition (nitrogen fixation and phosphate solubilization), production of plant growth promoting substances and inhibition of [plant pathogen](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/plant-pathogens "Learn more about plant pathogen from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") growth, and combining all these. Despite being cost-effective and environmentally benign, constraints like inconsistent supplies and a lack of sufficient [quality control](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/quality-control "Learn more about quality control from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is limiting the wide adoption or deployment of biofertilizers. To make them commercialy successful, we need to diversify resource base (i.e., identify and include more viable strains), create better manufacturing technology, and implement [quality control](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/quality-control "Learn more about quality control from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") procedures. Use of nanotechnology in biofertilizer sector via green synthesis or by nanocoating or encapsulation of [beneficial microorganism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/beneficial-microorganisms "Learn more about beneficial microorganism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using [nanomaterials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nanomaterial "Learn more about nanomaterials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a promising option. Bio-nanofertilizers increase [nutrient use efficiency](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/nutrient-use-efficiency "Learn more about nutrient use efficiency from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); diminishing nutrient losses, have multiple crop growth promoting activities and release of nutrients to rate compatible to plants demand. Adoption and popularization of bio-nanofertilizers require extensive field-testing, public awareness campaigns, capacity building of resource persons and stakeholders, adoptation of standard production processes, storage and application, as well as active participation of private organizations and enforcement of suitable legislation. Here, we discuss before-mentioned issues in light of available evidences and our experience with India system.

Introduction
------------

Biofertilizers are “An inocula having livings microbial entities that enhance the fertility of soil by adding nitrogen via biological fixation, or degrading and solubilizing complex organic product, promote plant growth and development by synthesizing plant grwoth promoting substances” (Okur, 2018). By the end of the nineteenth century, experiments relating to the use of both chemical and biological fertilizers had started. The idea of biological nitrogen fixation (BNF) was first proposed by the French chemist and agriculturist J.B. Boussingault in 1834 (Hellriegel and Wilfarth, 1888). In 1896, for the first time commercial production of rhizobium culture as biofertilizer, known as ‘Nitragin’ had begun (Nobbe and Hiltner, 1896). The ‘Chilean nitrate’ was the first chemical fertilizer used in agriculture during 1830–1840.

According to the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (UNFAO), the global population is projected to reach 9.8 billion by 2050 (Reetz, 2016). And, to meet the rising food demands of this population, we have to double the agricultural productivity (FAO, 2017; Nakai, 2018). Further, data from the Food Insecurity Experience Scale Report, suggests that one in three people are not getting sufficient food (nearly 928 million people; 26.6% of the global population). Compared to the previous five (22.6%) this increase in malnutrition particularly, in children would lead to stunting growth, low birth weight of infant, anaemic condition during pregnancy in mothers. Thus to increase the agriculture productivity and satisfy the global demands for nutritious food, we have to increase the soil fertility and nutrient use efficiency of the crops (Keane et al., 2009).

India is the second most populous nation (1.16 billion in 2011and is expected to reach 1.5 billion by 2025 AD) after china with only 2.4% of the global land area, of which, the total cultivated area is 193 million hectares, producing about 212 million tons food grain. We need to produce 310 million tonnes of food grains to feed the increasing population, or in other words we must double agriculture input in the next 30 years. Although, agricultural innovations have resulted in a fourfold rise in crop yield, however, the potential for increasing total cultivated area is limited. Thus, the only possible way is to expand vertically, supplementing soil with agricultural manures, composts, green manures and biofertilizers (Bagyaraj and Aparna, 2009). In India, the production of biofertilizer at commercial scale started in 1956. To give a boost to biofertilizers the Government of India launched a scheme named ‘National Project on Development and Use of Biofertilizer’ for their promotion, utilization, product distribution and training programme. Under the planning commission's 10 th plan period, ‘National Project on Organic Farming’ (NPOF) was made operational, with one national [National Centre of Organic Farming (NCOF)] and six regional centres [Regional Centres of Organic Farming (RCOF)]. Besides biofertilizers, use of organic manures, bio-control agents, and other organic materials had also been stressed on in the 10 th plan document, along with strengthening the vital infrastructures. All the sectors of Indian agriculture are now been encouraged to use biofertilizers in accordance to the principles of the Sustainable Agriculture.

Plants require a variety of nutrients of which 13 is obtained from the soil. They are taken up from the soil by the crop plants, and need to be replenished, usually by the application of chemical fertilizers. Production of synthetic fertilizers is petroleum dependent (a non-renewble source), harmful to the environment and human, and is costly. Worldwide, research on biofertilizers and their use for sustainable agriculture and environment is increasing day by day (Yadav and Sarkar, 2019). Nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) and potassium (K) are the major essential nutrients limiting the agricultural productivity. Continued and excessive usage of chemical fertilisers has resulted in environmental contamination causing ecological harm, pest resistance, human health issues, and decrease in soil fertility and crop output (Youssef and Eissa, 2014). Biofertilizers has long been suggested as a suitable replacement for chemical fertilizers, with capacity to extenuate the teething troubles associated with chemical fertilizers. To emerge as an alternative, biofertilizers must fulfill minimum necessities of primary level. Such as, majority of the farmers in developing country are of lower middle class cannot support the high cost of biofertilizers, so biofertilizers must be economic feasible. They must be practical to apply within a specific governmental system, conform to varied socio-cultural patterns; easy to use and reuse without requiring large additional inputs, and be productive for long-term agriculture (Suhag, 2016).

Biofertilizers are microorganisms based formulations. Microbes promote plant growth and development by fixing nitrogen, promotes phosphorus availability via its solubilization, and releasing growth promoting substances (Malusà et al., 2016). They are natural entities that ensure the availability of essential nutrients to plant from their natural unavailable source (Basu et al., 2021). They colonize the plants at rhizospheric, endophytic or epiphytic planes (Rossmann et al., 2017; Kumar et al., 2021). The latent or live cell applied on seed, seedling, or directly in soil promote the growth and development of crops via decomposing the organic matter, facilitating nutrients availability via secreting different type of enzymes or acids, producing growth promoting phytohormones, and strengthening defence mechanism against various biotic and abiotic factors (Fasusi et al., 2021; J. Zhang et al., 2021). The secretion of root exudates, which includes sugar molecules, fatty acids, organic acids, vitamins and other minearals (Sasse et al., 2018) accelerates proliferation of these microorganisms in rhizosphere. Up and down regulation of root exudates secretion would be a suitable strategy to ameliorate the microbial colonization in the rhizosperic region (Kawasaki et al., 2021). Various omics approaches could be used to traces microbial colonization ability at taxonomic, genetic and functional levels (Matthews et al., 2019; de Vries et al., 2020; Raghu et al., 2021; Gupta et al., 2021). Microbial fertilizers minimize the repeated application of chemical fertilizer by self-replicating and slowly release the available nutrients in soil (Wang et al., 2020, 2021, 2022; Tkacz et al., 2020).

Several beneficial strains of microbes have been isolated, some of which have considerable biotechnological potential (Nafis et al., 2019; Rai et al., 2018). The organisms typically utilised as a component of biofertilizers are nitrogen fixers, potassium and phosphorus solubilizers, or their mixtures with moulds and fungus (Pathak and Kumar, 2016). Microbes isolated from rhizosphere are the most sught after for biofertilizer (Xiang et al., 2012), and are being sold as biofertilizers in America, Europe, and somewhat in Asian countries (Mishra et al., 2017). Destpite many benefits, major bottleneck in famers hesitancy to the use of biofertilizers is quality and efficacy of products resulting from poor investment in research and development partticlarly in bioformulation process (Thompson, 1991; Stamenković et al., 2018; Shravani, 2019).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biofertilizer production is often more expensive than chemical fertilizers due to specialized processes, quality control, and the need for specific microbial strains, limiting market competitiveness.
Many consumers remain doubtful about the efficacy and safety of biofertilizers, stemming from past experiences with chemical products, necessitating enhanced education and transparency in marketing.

A robust marketing strategy is essential to communicate the benefits of biofertilizers, addressing misconceptions, highlighting environmental advantages, and improving consumer trust to drive adoption.
